museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: Pitfall! swings onto the Atari 2600, Joust lifts off in arcades, Pole Position defines the racing cabinet, and the UK micro shelf keeps filling with cassette possibilities.
museum.curatorNote: September 1982 is unusually strong for arcade and Atari 2600 anchors, but regional rollouts still varied. This exhibit uses exact days only where the sources are unusually clear, and otherwise keeps to month-level dating.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: September puts motion and mastery on display: running, flying, racing, loading.

items.title: Pitfall! becomes Activision's jungle showcase
items.body: David Crane's Atari 2600 adventure showed how much personality could be coaxed from the ageing console when design, animation and marketing lined up.
items.sourceNote: MobyGames and other release references list Pitfall! for Atari 2600 in September 1982.

items.title: Joust reaches arcades
items.body: Williams turned flapping momentum into a competitive ritual: two riders, awkward physics, eggs to snatch, and a cabinet that looked simpler than it played.
items.sourceNote: Joust references list the arcade release in September 1982.

items.title: Pole Position launches in Japan
items.body: Namco's racing cabinet brought a convincing track, qualifying lap structure and billboard-bright spectacle to the arcade floor. Its wider Western story follows later in the year.
items.sourceNote: Pole Position sources list Japan release on 16 September 1982, with North American mass-market release later.

items.title: Third-party console games look like the creative edge
items.body: Activision's success made cartridges feel less like machine-owner accessories and more like authored works by named designers.
items.sourceNote: Built from Pitfall!'s September release and Activision's 1982 market position.

items.title: British micro owners keep learning through magazines
items.body: For UK readers, the home-computer future was still half shop window and half printed page: reviews, type-ins, adverts and cassette offers shaping what games could be at home.
items.sourceNote: Included as UK microcomputer magazine culture rather than a single dated claim.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: Eight releases and release-window objects from a month where arcade craft and home-console design both mattered.

items.title: Pitfall!
items.body: A side-on jungle treasure hunt that became one of the defining Atari 2600 originals.
items.sourceNote: MobyGames lists September 1982 for Atari 2600.

items.title: Joust
items.body: Williams' physics-led contest about altitude, timing and risky landings.
items.sourceNote: Joust release references list September 1982.

items.title: Pole Position
items.body: Namco's racing hit begins in Japan before its later North American mass release.
items.sourceNote: Pole Position release summaries list Japan on September 16, 1982.

items.title: Donkey Kong Jr.
items.body: Still fresh in cabinets after its August release, keeping Nintendo's character-platformer moment alive.
items.sourceNote: August 1982 release; included as September arcade-floor context.

items.title: Dig Dug
items.body: Namco's tunnelling game remained part of the 1982 arcade conversation as operators refreshed floors through the year.
items.sourceNote: February 1982 Japan release; month here is contextual.

items.title: Zaxxon
items.body: Sega's isometric shooter continued to make diagonals and shadows feel like arcade technology from the future.
items.sourceNote: January 1982 release anchor; included as ongoing arcade presence.

items.title: Spectrum cassette games
items.body: The young UK machine's software library kept growing through small publishers, listings and mail-order discovery.
items.sourceNote: No specific September title date asserted.

items.title: Atari 2600 back-catalogue buying
items.body: Pitfall! arrived onto a shelf already crowded with arcade licences, sports carts and older family favourites.
items.sourceNote: Market context around Atari 2600 and Activision in 1982.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: The machines explain the mood: old console, new skill, fresh cabinets, young micros.

items.title: Atari 2600
items.body: Pitfall! made the 2600 feel capable again, proving that expressive animation and clever level structure could refresh familiar hardware.
items.sourceNote: Anchored to Pitfall!'s September release.

items.title: Williams arcade hardware
items.body: Joust's cabinet turned a two-button rule set into something that felt physical, awkward and theatrical.
items.sourceNote: Joust arcade release context.

items.title: Namco Pole Position cabinet
items.body: Pole Position's steering wheel, pedal and track perspective made racing feel closer to a fairground attraction than a domestic game.
items.sourceNote: Pole Position September Japan release context.

items.title: ZX Spectrum and UK cassette machines
items.body: In Britain, games increasingly meant a home micro connected to the family television, with software arriving as tape and paper as often as boxed cartridges.
items.sourceNote: UK microcomputer context around the ZX Spectrum launch year.

sections.title: Magazine Covers
sections.subtitle: The printed shelf made September's games legible: what to buy, what to type, what to play next.

items.title: Pitfall! as console event
items.headline: Original cartridge design becomes the story
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed Electronic Games cover for September 1982.
items.curatorNote: Pitfall! suited a magazine culture beginning to recognise named designers and original console work.
items.body: Pitfall! suited a magazine culture beginning to recognise named designers and original console work.
items.sourceNote: Specific September cover line not asserted.

items.title: C&VG arcade watch
items.headline: Joust, racing cabinets and the arcade floor
items.curatorNote: For UK readers, arcade coverage connected local cabinets to the wider American and Japanese trade.
items.body: For UK readers, arcade coverage connected local cabinets to the wider American and Japanese trade.
items.sourceNote: Specific cover line not asserted.

items.title: Popular Computing Weekly
items.headline: Listings, adverts and new-machine nerves
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed Popular Computing Weekly cover for September 1982.
items.curatorNote: The weekly press gave British micro owners a rolling sense of movement before software shops standardised everything.
items.body: The weekly press gave British micro owners a rolling sense of movement before software shops standardised everything.
items.sourceNote: Specific issue line not asserted.

items.title: Personal Computer World
items.headline: Home computing becomes a family decision
items.curatorNote: Games lived inside a broader home-computer buying conversation about memory, keyboards and education.
items.body: Games lived inside a broader home-computer buying conversation about memory, keyboards and education.
items.sourceNote: Specific cover line not asserted.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: The network was the cabinet queue, the letters page and the post.

items.title: High scores were local memory
items.body: Joust and Pole Position scores travelled by witness, initials and boast.

items.title: Design reputation moved through magazines
items.body: Activision's designer-forward image depended on print as much as packaging.

items.title: Type-ins still meant labour
items.body: UK micro owners discovered games by typing them from paper and debugging their own mistakes.

items.title: Buying advice was social
items.body: A friend who had actually seen Pitfall! or a new arcade cabinet carried unusual authority.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: September 1982 felt like skill was being redefined in several directions at once.

items.title: Pitfall! made the living room feel authored
items.body: It was not just another imitation of a cabinet. It felt designed for the console in front of you.

items.title: Joust made awkwardness elegant
items.body: The strange flap of the controls became the whole point, turning embarrassment into mastery.

items.title: Pole Position made speed theatrical
items.body: Even seeing the cabinet was enough to make older racing games feel suddenly flat.

items.title: The UK home future was still homemade
items.body: A game might be bought, copied from a page, loaded from tape, or half-imagined from an advert.
